1:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," The very brief verse notes the LORD speaking concerning purification after childbirth. The grammatical voices note the introduction. Henry and Gill note that "the LORD spake unto Moses, saying" — the LORD spoke to Moses, now to give the law of purification after childbirth (vv.2-8); the speaking (God's command, the new law of the woman's purifying); the occasion (after the laws of clean and unclean foods, ch. 11, now the cleansing after birth, the continuation of the laws of purity). That the LORD himself opens this law shows that even the natural process of birth was brought under his ordering, the holiness of his people reaching into the most ordinary acts of life.

On "the LORD spake unto Moses," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the word. Calvin and Ellicott note the LORD speaking (the law of purification, the cleansing of the woman after childbirth) — the new law (the purifying after birth, the laws of cleanness continuing).

On the speaking, the grammatical-historical note the command: the LORD spake unto Moses — the law of purification introduced; the providence (God ordering the cleansing after birth, the holiness of the people in all things). The verse displays the LORD speaking concerning purification after childbirth.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the introduction — "the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," the purification (vv.2-8, Henry, Gill); "the LORD spake unto Moses," the word (Calvin, Ellicott); the command (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the speaking LORD. The verse marks the speaking: God's. The verse displays the purification: introduced. The verse displays the authority: divine. The verse displays the cleansing: ordered. The speaking displays God ordering the cleansing after birth. The verse displays the LORD: speaking. The verse displays the law: of purifying. The verse displays the holiness: in all things. The LORD speaking concerning purification after childbirth displays the divine command: the LORD spake unto Moses (vv.2-8) — God giving the law of purification after birth, the holiness of the people reaching into the ordinary acts of life. The verse displays the uncleanness of childbirth (v.2).
2:
"Speak unto the children of Israel, saying, If a woman have conceived seed, and born a man child: then she shall be unclean seven days; according to the days of the separation for her infirmity shall she be unclean." The witnesses note the uncleanness of childbirth — the crux of inherited corruption. On "If a woman have conceived seed, and born a man child: then she shall be unclean seven days," interpreters expound the uncleanness. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the meaning — "she shall be unclean seven days": a woman who bore a child was ceremonially unclean (seven days for a son, two weeks for a daughter, vv.5), as in her monthly separation; the witnesses note the significance — the uncleanness not because childbirth is sinful (it is the fulfilling of God's command, "be fruitful and multiply," and a blessing, Psalm 127:3), but a ceremonial figure; the lesson — the figure of inherited corruption (the uncleanness a token of the common corruption of our nature, that we are conceived and born in sin, the child brought forth a sinner, the taint of original sin passed down; "behold, I was shapen in iniquity; and in sin did my mother conceive me," Psalm 51:5; "who can bring a clean thing out of an unclean? not one," Job 14:4; "that which is born of the flesh is flesh," John 3:6); the lesson — the need of cleansing from the very beginning, the inherited defilement requiring atonement (vv.6-8); the figure of original sin, that even the holy and blessed act of birth brings forth a child needing redemption.

On "according to the days of the separation for her infirmity shall she be unclean," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the separation. Wesley and Keil note the uncleanness as in the monthly separation (the ceremonial impurity, the figure of the corruption of nature) — the figure (the inherited taint, the need of cleansing).

On the uncleanness, the grammatical-historical note the corruption: the unclean seven days — the figure of inherited sin; the providence (the uncleanness a token of original corruption, the need of atonement, Psalm 51:5). The verse displays the uncleanness of childbirth.

The witnesses agree the substance: the woman was unclean after childbirth, a ceremonial figure of inherited corruption (Calvin, Wesley, Keil, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of inherited corruption: the uncleanness of childbirth — not because birth is sinful (it is a blessing and God's command), but a figure of the common corruption of our nature, that we are conceived and born in sin (Psalm 51:5; Job 14:4; John 3:6), the child brought forth a sinner needing cleansing from the beginning, a figure of original sin and the need of redemption. The verse displays the inherited-corruption crux. The verse marks the woman: unclean. The verse displays the birth: a figure. The verse displays the corruption: inherited. The verse displays the cleansing: needed. The uncleanness displays the figure of inherited sin. The verse displays the child: born in sin. The verse displays the corruption: passed down. The verse displays the redemption: needed. The uncleanness of childbirth displays the crux: she shall be unclean (Psalm 51:5; Job 14:4; John 3:6) — the figure of the common corruption of our nature, that we are conceived and born in sin, the child needing cleansing and redemption from the beginning. The verse displays circumcision on the eighth day (v.3).
3:
"And in the eighth day the flesh of his foreskin shall be circumcised." The witnesses note circumcision on the eighth day — the crux of the covenant sign. On "in the eighth day the flesh of his foreskin shall be circumcised," interpreters expound the rite. The reformed and the dominant reading note circumcision — "in the eighth day the flesh of his foreskin shall be circumcised": the male child was to be circumcised on the eighth day; the witnesses note the significance — circumcision the sign of the covenant (the token of God's covenant with Abraham and his seed, Genesis 17:10-14, "this is my covenant... every man child among you shall be circumcised... and it shall be a token of the covenant"); the eighth day (the appointed day, after the seven days of the mother's first uncleanness, the child now received into the covenant; the Lord himself circumcised the eighth day, Luke 2:21); the lesson — the cutting away of the flesh, the figure of the putting off of the corruption of nature; the figure — the circumcision of the heart, the inward reality the outward sign pointed to (Deuteronomy 30:6, "the LORD thy God will circumcise thine heart"; Romans 2:28-29, "circumcision is that of the heart, in the spirit"; Colossians 2:11, "in whom also ye are circumcised with the circumcision made without hands, in putting off the body of the sins of the flesh by the circumcision of Christ"); the putting off of the flesh, the dying to sin, the new birth; the seal of the covenant righteousness (Romans 4:11, circumcision "a seal of the righteousness of the faith").

On "the flesh of his foreskin shall be circumcised," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the sign. Ellicott and Clarke note the circumcision (the covenant sign, the cutting away of the flesh, Genesis 17:10-14) — the figure (the putting off of the flesh, the circumcision of the heart, Colossians 2:11); the providence (the covenant sign, the inward reality fulfilled in Christ). The verse displays circumcision on the eighth day.

The witnesses agree the substance: the male child was circumcised on the eighth day, the covenant sign (Ellicott, Clarke, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the covenant sign: circumcision on the eighth day — the sign and token of God's covenant with Abraham and his seed (Genesis 17:10-14), the child received into the covenant (the Lord himself circumcised the eighth day, Luke 2:21), the cutting away of the flesh a figure of the circumcision of the heart and the putting off of the corruption of nature (Deuteronomy 30:6; Romans 2:28-29; Colossians 2:11), the seal of the righteousness of faith (Romans 4:11). The verse displays the covenant-sign crux. The verse marks the circumcision: the eighth day. The verse displays the covenant: the sign. The verse displays the flesh: cut away. The verse displays the heart: circumcised. The rite displays the covenant sign and the circumcision of the heart. The verse displays the sign: the covenant. The verse displays the flesh: put off. The verse displays the Christ: the true circumcision. Circumcision on the eighth day displays the crux: in the eighth day... circumcised (Genesis 17:10-14; Colossians 2:11; Romans 4:11) — the covenant sign, the cutting away of the flesh, a figure of the circumcision of the heart and the putting off of the corruption of nature in Christ. The verse displays the days of purifying (vv.4-5).
4:
"And she shall then continue in the blood of her purifying three and thirty days; she shall touch no hallowed thing, nor come into the sanctuary, until the days of her purifying be fulfilled." The witnesses note the days of purifying and the woman kept from the sanctuary. On "she shall then continue in the blood of her purifying three and thirty days," the grammatical voices note the days. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "she shall then continue in the blood of her purifying three and thirty days; she shall touch no hallowed thing, nor come into the sanctuary, until the days of her purifying be fulfilled" — after the seven days of full uncleanness (vv.2), the woman continued thirty-three more days in her purifying (forty days in all for a son), during which she touched no holy thing nor came to the sanctuary; the thirty-three days (the longer period of purifying, the recovery and cleansing); the touch no hallowed thing (the woman kept from the holy things and the sanctuary until cleansed); the figure (the period of cleansing required, the holiness guarded).

On "she shall touch no hallowed thing, nor come into the sanctuary," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the separation. Calvin and Ellicott note the woman kept from the sanctuary and the holy things (until the purifying was fulfilled) — the holiness (the holy things not approached in uncleanness, the reverence required, vv.7); the figure (the cleansing required before approaching God).

On "until the days of her purifying be fulfilled," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the fulfilling: "until the days of her purifying be fulfilled" — the days completed (the cleansing finished, then the offering, vv.6); the figure (the period of cleansing, the reverence for the holy); the providence (the purifying fulfilled, the offering to follow). The verse displays the days of purifying and the woman kept from the sanctuary.

The witnesses agree the substance: the woman continued thirty-three days in her purifying, kept from the sanctuary until cleansed (Calvin, Wesley, Keil, Gill). The verse bears its weight: the days of purifying — the longer period of cleansing (forty days for a son), during which the woman touched no holy thing nor came to the sanctuary, the holiness of the holy things guarded (the cleansing required before approaching God), a figure of the period of purifying and the reverence due to the holy. The verse displays the days of purifying. The verse marks the days: thirty-three. The verse displays the woman: kept from the sanctuary. The verse displays the holy: not touched. The verse displays the cleansing: required. The days display the period of purifying. The verse displays the woman: cleansing. The verse displays the sanctuary: not approached. The verse displays the holiness: guarded. The days of purifying and the woman kept from the sanctuary display the cleansing required: thirty-three days, touch no hallowed thing (vv.6-7) — the period of purifying, the woman kept from the holy things until cleansed, the reverence due to the holy. The verse displays the law for a daughter (v.5).
5:
"But if she bear a maid child, then she shall be unclean two weeks, as in her separation: and she shall continue in the blood of her purifying threescore and six days." The very brief verse notes the doubled days of purifying for a daughter. The grammatical voices note the difference. Ellicott and Gill note that "if she bear a maid child, then she shall be unclean two weeks, as in her separation: and she shall continue in the blood of her purifying threescore and six days" — for a daughter, the uncleanness and purifying were doubled (two weeks of full uncleanness and sixty-six more days, eighty days in all); the doubled days (twice the period for a son); the as in her separation (the uncleanness as the monthly); the figure (the longer period for a daughter, variously explained — the witnesses note it is not because the female is more sinful, but perhaps a ceremonial distinction; the corruption of nature inherited by all alike, the cleansing required for both).

On "she shall be unclean two weeks, as in her separation," the witnesses note the doubling. Gill notes the doubled uncleanness for a daughter (twice the days, the ceremonial distinction) — the figure (the inherited corruption, the cleansing for both).

On "she shall continue in the blood of her purifying threescore and six days," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the period: "she shall continue in the blood of her purifying threescore and six days" — the eighty days in all (the longer purifying for a daughter); the figure (the cleansing required, the corruption inherited by all); the providence (the period of purifying, the offering to follow, vv.6). The verse displays the doubled days of purifying for a daughter.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the difference — "if she bear a maid child, then she shall be unclean two weeks... and she shall continue in the blood of her purifying threescore and six days," the doubling (Ellicott, Gill); "she shall be unclean two weeks, as in her separation," the doubling (Gill); "she shall continue in the blood of her purifying threescore and six days," the period (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the doubled days. The verse marks the days: doubled. The verse displays the daughter: the longer purifying. The verse displays the uncleanness: two weeks. The verse displays the cleansing: required. The doubled days display the longer purifying for a daughter. The verse displays the days: eighty. The verse displays the purifying: doubled. The verse displays the corruption: inherited by all. The doubled days of purifying for a daughter display the cleansing required: two weeks unclean, sixty-six days more — the longer purifying for a daughter, the ceremonial distinction, the corruption inherited by all and the cleansing required. The verse displays the offering of purification (vv.6-7).
6:
"And when the days of her purifying are fulfilled, for a son, or for a daughter, she shall bring a lamb of the first year for a burnt offering, and a young pigeon, or a turtledove, for a sin offering, unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, unto the priest:" The witnesses note the offering of purification — the crux of the need of atonement. On "she shall bring a lamb of the first year for a burnt offering, and a young pigeon, or a turtledove, for a sin offering," interpreters expound the offering. The reformed and the dominant reading note the offerings — "a lamb... for a burnt offering, and a young pigeon, or a turtledove, for a sin offering": when the days were fulfilled, the woman brought a lamb for a burnt offering and a bird for a sin offering; the witnesses note the significance — the offering of atonement after childbirth (the sin offering for the cleansing, the burnt offering for the devotion); the lesson — the need of atonement even after the blessed act of birth (the uncleanness, the figure of inherited corruption, requiring a sin offering, the conscience cleansed; "by one man sin entered into the world," Romans 5:12; the need of atonement from the very entrance into the world); the burnt offering (the devotion and thanksgiving for the child); the figure — the sin offering and burnt offering, the atonement and the devotion, fulfilled in Christ; and the remarkable witness — the Lord's own mother brought this offering after his birth (Luke 2:22-24, "when the days of her purification according to the law of Moses were accomplished, they brought him to Jerusalem... and to offer a sacrifice"), Mary submitting to the law, and the sinless Christ brought under the law born of a woman (Galatians 4:4).

On "for a burnt offering... for a sin offering," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the two offerings. Wesley and Keil note the burnt offering (the devotion) and the sin offering (the atonement) — the figure (the devotion and the atonement, fulfilled in Christ); the need (the cleansing required). The verse displays the offering of purification.

The witnesses agree the substance: the woman brought a lamb for a burnt offering and a bird for a sin offering when the days were fulfilled (Wesley, Keil, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the need of atonement: the offering of purification — the sin offering for cleansing and the burnt offering for devotion after childbirth, the need of atonement even after the blessed act of birth (the figure of inherited corruption, Romans 5:12), fulfilled in Christ; and the witness that the Lord's own mother brought this offering after his birth (Luke 2:22-24), Mary submitting to the law, the sinless Christ born of a woman, made under the law (Galatians 4:4). The verse displays the atonement crux. The verse marks the offerings: burnt and sin. The verse displays the atonement: for cleansing. The verse displays the devotion: the burnt offering. The verse displays the Christ: born under the law. The offering displays the need of atonement after birth. The verse displays the sin offering: cleansing. The verse displays the Mary: offering. The verse displays the Christ: under the law. The offering of purification displays the crux: a lamb for a burnt offering, a bird for a sin offering (Romans 5:12; Luke 2:22-24; Galatians 4:4) — the need of atonement even after childbirth, fulfilled in Christ, whose own mother brought this very offering. The verse displays the atonement made and the cleansing (v.7).
7:
"Who shall offer it before the LORD, and make an atonement for her; and she shall be cleansed from the issue of her blood. This is the law for her that hath born a male or a female." The very brief verse notes the atonement made and the woman cleansed. The grammatical voices note the atonement. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "who shall offer it before the LORD, and make an atonement for her; and she shall be cleansed from the issue of her blood. This is the law for her that hath born a male or a female" — the priest offered the sacrifice, made atonement, and the woman was cleansed; the atonement (the covering by the sacrifice, the cleansing); the cleansed (the woman purified, restored to the holy things and the sanctuary); the the law (the same law for a son or a daughter, the cleansing for both); the figure (the atonement and cleansing, fulfilled in Christ, Hebrews 9:14).

On "make an atonement for her; and she shall be cleansed," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the cleansing. Calvin and Ellicott note the atonement and cleansing (the woman purified by the sacrifice, restored) — the figure (the cleansing through the atonement, the conscience purged, Hebrews 9:14, "the blood of Christ... purge your conscience").

On "This is the law for her that hath born a male or a female," the grammatical-historical note the law: "This is the law for her that hath born a male or a female" — the same law for both (the cleansing for son or daughter); the figure (the corruption inherited by all, the cleansing for all); the providence (the atonement and cleansing, the law for all). The verse displays the atonement made and the woman cleansed.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the atonement — "who shall offer it before the LORD, and make an atonement for her; and she shall be cleansed... This is the law," the atonement and cleansing (Hebrews 9:14, Keil, Gill); "make an atonement for her; and she shall be cleansed," the cleansing (Calvin, Ellicott); "This is the law for her that hath born a male or a female," the law (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the cleansing. The verse marks the atonement: made. The verse displays the woman: cleansed. The verse displays the law: for both. The verse displays the Christ: the atonement. The atonement displays the cleansing through the sacrifice. The verse displays the atonement: by blood. The verse displays the cleansing: granted. The verse displays the Christ: who purges. The atonement made and the woman cleansed display the cleansing: make atonement, and she shall be cleansed (Hebrews 9:14) — the atonement by the sacrifice and the woman purified, the figure of the cleansing of the conscience through the blood of Christ. The verse displays the provision for the poor (v.8).
8:
"And if she be not able to bring a lamb, then she shall bring two turtles, or two young pigeons; the one for the burnt offering, and the other for a sin offering: and the priest shall make an atonement for her, and she shall be clean." The witnesses note the provision for the poor — the crux of God's mercy and the Lord's own poor mother. On "if she be not able to bring a lamb, then she shall bring two turtles, or two young pigeons," interpreters expound the provision. The dominant reading and the grammatical voices note the mercy — "if she be not able to bring a lamb, then she shall bring two turtles, or two young pigeons": if the woman was too poor to bring a lamb, she might bring two birds (one for the burnt offering, one for the sin offering); the witnesses note the significance — the gracious provision for the poor (the way of cleansing adapted to the offerer's ability, the poor not excluded for want of means, the same atonement and cleansing for the poor); the lesson — God's mercy to the poor (the atonement made accessible to all, the lamb or, if too poor, the birds); the figure — the remarkable witness that the Lord's own mother brought this poor's offering after his birth (Luke 2:24, "a pair of turtledoves, or two young pigeons"), Mary being poor, and the Lord of glory born into a poor family that brought the offering of the poor, the King of kings entering the world in poverty (2 Corinthians 8:9, "though he was rich, yet for your sakes he became poor"); the accessibility of cleansing to all, and the deep poverty of Christ's earthly estate.

On "the one for the burnt offering, and the other for a sin offering," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the two birds. Wesley and Ellicott note the two birds (one for the burnt offering, one for the sin offering, the devotion and the atonement) — the figure (the atonement for the poor, the same cleansing).

On "the priest shall make an atonement for her, and she shall be clean," the grammatical-historical note the cleansing: "the priest shall make an atonement for her, and she shall be clean" — the atonement and cleansing for the poor (the same as the rich, the equal pardon); the figure (the cleansing through the atonement, accessible to all); the providence (the poor cleansed, the mercy of God). The verse displays the provision for the poor.

The witnesses agree the substance: if too poor for a lamb, the woman brought two birds, and was cleansed (Wesley, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of God's mercy and the Lord's own poor mother: the provision for the poor — the gracious provision for the poor (the way of cleansing adapted to ability, none excluded by poverty, the same atonement for the poor), and the remarkable witness that the Lord's own mother brought this very poor's offering after his birth (Luke 2:24), Mary being poor, the Lord of glory born into poverty (2 Corinthians 8:9), the accessibility of cleansing to all. The verse displays the poor-provision crux. The verse marks the birds: for the poor. The verse displays the cleansing: accessible. The provision displays God's mercy to the poor, the very offering the Lord's poor mother brought. The provision for the poor displays the crux: if not able to bring a lamb, two turtles or two pigeons (Luke 2:24; 2 Corinthians 8:9) — the gracious provision for the poor, the cleansing accessible to all, the very offering the Lord's poor mother brought, the Lord of glory born into poverty. The verse closes the chapter, before the law of leprosy (chs.13-14).
